articles.title: Milbank Bumped Salaries! — See Also
articles.content:
我们喜欢看到 associate 的薪酬！：[米尔班克律师事务所再次提高 associate 薪资](https://abovethelaw.com/2026/06/alert-milbank-does-it-again-associate-salaries-are-going-up/)。

我们有匹配了！：[McDermott Will & Schulte 成为首家匹配米尔班克薪资标准的律师事务所](https://abovethelaw.com/2026/06/the-salary-wars-have-begun-first-firm-matches-new-biglaw-pay-scale/)！

托德·布兰奇被莫斯了：[司法部针对科米的86项起诉以零结果告终](https://abovethelaw.com/2026/06/doj-just-got-mossed-on-86-47-legal-theory/)。

律师事务所正在大量购买房地产：[大型律师事务所的办公空间正在推动本世纪最好的房地产热潮](https://abovethelaw.com/2026/06/biglaw-office-space-driving-best-real-estate-boom-this-century/)！令人惊叹！不过不确定为什么在采用人工智能后他们还需要那么多空间。

不妨跳过办公时间：[ChatGPT 刚刚取代了法学院的办公时间？](https://abovethelaw.com/2026/06/did-chat-gpt-replace-law-school-office-hours/)双盲研究表明，人工智能在回答问题方面可能比教授更有帮助。

这篇文章首次出现在[Above the Law](https://abovethelaw.com)网站上，标题为[Milbank Bumped Salaries! — See Also](https://abovethelaw.com/2026/06/milbank-bumped-salaries-see-also/)。
articles.summary:
We Love To See The Associate Compensation!: And Milbank loves setting the scale.

We Have A Match!: McDermott Will & Schulte the first to match the Milbank scale!

Todd Blanche Got Mossed: The DOJ’s Comey 86 prosecutions comes up with goose egg.

Law Firms Are Buying Up Real Estate: Remarkable! Not sure why they’ll need all that space with AI adoption, though.

Might As Well Skip Office Hours: Double blind study suggests AI may be more helpful than your professors at answering questions.

The post Milbank Bumped Salaries! — See Also appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Ironclad Founder Jason Boehmig Joins OpenAI To Develop Products For The Legal Sector
articles.content:
法律行业正成为两家主导性前沿人工智能公司之间的战场。就在不到一个月前，Anthropic公司（Claude AI助手的开发商）迈出了进入法律市场的重要一步，发布了20多个MCP连接器，将Claude与律师事务所和企业法务部门使用的软件连接起来，同时还推出了12个针对特定法律实践领域的插件。

如今，其最大竞争对手、ChatGPT开发商OpenAI已聘请杰森·伯姆尼克（Jason Boehmig）担任法律行业产品开发负责人。伯姆尼克昨天在LinkedIn上宣布了这一消息。

伯姆尼克曾是芬威克-韦斯特律师事务所（Fenwick & West）的公司律师，2014年与前Palantir Technologies软件工程师王维（Cai Wangwilt）共同创立了合同生命周期管理公司Ironclad。他曾在2021年做客我的LawNext播客节目。

他于2025年辞去CEO职务，但在此之前，他已见证公司估值达到32亿美元。根据他的LinkedIn个人资料，他目前仍担任Ironclad的执行董事长和董事会成员。

以下是他在LinkedIn上的发文内容：

今天我满怀希望。这是我加入OpenAI的第一天，负责法律垂直领域的产品工作。

我感到很幸运能来到这里。十二年前，我辞去了律师事务所助理律师的工作，创办了一家名为Ironclad的公司。当时我是一名孤独的创始人，背负着20万美元的学生贷款，而且几乎不会编程。我所处的领域被投资者视为荒地——开发法律技术，向律师销售产品。感觉所有不利因素都针对我而来。

但我有一个巨大优势，那就是一小群相信我的人。内尔·牛顿（Nell Newton）在法学院期间鼓励我探索对技术的兴趣，并帮助我在硅谷打开了局面。特德·王（Ted Wang）敢于押注一个想要自动化自己工作的另类律师。杰西·贝鲁特伊（Jesse Beyroutey）有勇气和远见，在没人认为这是个好主意之前，率先向公司投资。

如今，Ironclad为欧莱雅、壳牌和《纽约时报》等不同行业的领军企业管理着数十亿美元的商业合同。公司已从我在波特雷罗山的旧公寓起步，发展为拥有700多名优秀员工的全球企业，实现数亿美元的经常性收入，并通过在数据和工作流程中部署人工智能，持续快速增长。公司还拥有科技行业最优秀的CEO之一丹·斯普林格（Dan Springer）。没有他和团队的出色表现，我不可能迈出这一步。

当我审视今天的法律行业时，它比十二年前更加充满活力。律师事务所领导者正在为未来一百年重新构建其组织架构。总法律顾问和法律运营领导者正在推动人工智能应用的极限。州律师协会和公益法律组织正在寻找负责任且安全地弥合司法公正差距的方法。法学院正在重新思考法律教育。还有成千上万蓬勃发展的法律科技初创公司。这是一个令人兴奋的时代。

在很多方面，我在OpenAI想做的就是像内尔、特德和杰西当年相信我一样，去相信这个生态系统。认为任何单一参与者能够独自完成这项工作，即使是前沿实验室，也是一种错误。因此，我想听听你们这些构建者的声音。我一直很钦佩律师所具备的创造力和公民责任感。我知道，我们可以共同利用人工智能的力量，使其成为造福整个社会的力量，而不仅仅是顶层社会的工具。

我想不出还有什么比这更值得我投入的工作了。
articles.summary: The post Ironclad Founder Jason Boehmig Joins OpenAI To Develop Products For The Legal Sector appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: The Associate Compensation Race Is On With Latest Set Of Raises
articles.content:
多米诺骨牌开始倒下了，诸位。

紧随Milbank宣布将于7月1日起给律师涨薪10,000至20,000美元之后，加州和纽约诉讼精品律所Hueston Hennigan挺身而出，匹配了新的薪资标准。在你们问之前，这是在HH已经宣布的最高35,000美元夏季奖金基础上的额外加薪。

让我们花点时间欣赏正在发生的事情。Hueston Hennigan这家在薪酬大战中素来以小搏大的律所，不仅匹配了Milbank的新薪资标准，还同时让律师们已经坐拥10,000至35,000美元的夏季奖金（前提是律师需达到2,000小时的可计费工时要求）。对HH律师来说，这是一个极其美好的夏天。

宣布加薪的全所邮件：

这非常符合这家在薪酬话题上历来敢于发声的律所的风格。这也引出了一个显而易见的问题：下一个是谁？Milbank宣布新标准总是发令枪响起，而HH（以及McDermott Will & Schulte）如此迅速地回应（加上已经宣布的夏季奖金这一额外背景），将给所有希望在顶尖人才竞争中占据优势的律所施加压力。

各位请记住，我们依靠你们的线索来追踪薪酬动态，所以当你们的律所宣布或匹配新标准时，请给我们发短信（646-820-8477）或发邮件（主题行："[律所名称] 奖金/匹配"）。如果有的话，请附上备忘录。如果不想转发原始PDF或Word文件，你可以拍照通过短信或邮件发送。

Kathryn Rubino是Above the Law的高级编辑、The Jabot播客主持人，以及Thinking Like A Lawyer的联合主持人。AtL的爆料人是最棒的，所以请与她联系。欢迎通过邮件与她分享任何线索、问题或意见，并在Twitter @Kathryn1或Bluesky @Kathryn1上关注她。

《律师薪酬竞赛随着最新一轮加薪正式启动》一文首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
The dominoes are starting to fall, people.

Hot on the heels of Milbank’s announcement that associates would be seeing raises of $10,000 to $20,000 effective July 1, California and New York litigation boutique Hueston Hennigan has stepped up and matched the new scale. And before you ask, yes, this is in addition to the summer bonuses of up to $35,000 that HH already announced.

Let’s take a moment to appreciate what’s happening here. Hueston Hennigan, a firm well known for punching above its weight in the compensation wars, is not just matching the new Milbank salary scale, but doing so while associates are already sitting on summer bonuses that range from $10,000 to $35,000 (with the caveat that attorneys must be on track to bill 2,000 hours to be eligible). That is an extremely good summer to be an HH associate.

The firmwide email announcing the raises:

This is very much on brand for a firm that has a history of making noise in the compensation conversation. And it raises the obvious question: who’s next? Milbank announcing a new scale is always the opening bell, and HH (and McDermott Will & Schulte) answering it this quickly (plus the added context of already-announced summer bonuses) is going to put pressure on every other firm that wants to compete for elite talent.

Remember everyone, we depend on your tips to stay on top of compensation updates, so when your firm announces or matches, please text us (646-820-8477) or email us (subject line: “[Firm Name] Bonus/Matches”). Please include the memo if available. You can take a photo of the memo and send it via text or email if you don’t want to forward the original PDF or Word file.

Kathryn Rubino is a Senior Editor at Above the Law, host of The Jabot podcast, and co-host of Thinking Like A Lawyer. AtL tipsters are the best, so please connect with her. Feel free to email her with any tips, questions, or comments and follow her on Twitter @Kathryn1 or Bluesky @Kathryn1

The post The Associate Compensation Race Is On With Latest Set Of Raises appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: [A]ll [I] Could Ask For
articles.content:
这是一周内来自一位老客户的第二个非知识产权请求，通过其新任内部法律顾问提出。这两项请求中的第一项处理起来相当简单，尽管涉及一个已立案的诉讼。我和该客户几年前曾成功让一个类似案件在答辩前就被驳回。我需要做的只是回顾与那场纠纷相关的往来邮件。当我重新想起之前那个案件中我们所做的调查后，就能指导客户的新任内部法务人员了解他们需要从业务部门和一个相关外部供应商那里获取哪些信息。有了这些信息后，向对方律师提交并说服他们同意撤案就变得轻而易举了。这一次，我们甚至不需要像第一次那样聘请具有此类诉讼专业知识的顶级律所联合代理。这说明长期客户与律师之间的关系以及机构知识是有价值的。

然而，第二个事项就比较棘手了。首先，客户收到潜在索赔通知已有一段时间，回复时间所剩无几。更复杂的是，客户从供应商处获得赔偿的可能性几乎为零，因为该产品已经停产，最近的销售只是试图清理剩余库存。此外，原告律师威胁要在远离客户所在地的州法院提起诉讼，这意味着我需要帮助客户在我不熟悉的法律领域物色有能力的当地律师。吞下这剂苦药对我来说并不是什么诱人的选择，尤其是客户要求我立即协助准备最后一次庭外和解努力——通过与原告律师安排好的电话会议解决此事。由于时间紧迫，我快速阅读了对方律师的请求函，并与客户跟进确认了函中提出的一个事实问题。由于我对这类索赔不熟悉，我计划建议客户向人打听在纠纷所在州有办事处的律所推荐。

事实上，这是我通过电子邮件向客户提出的初步建议。值得称赞的是，客户要求更详细的指导，这促使我尝试用人工智能来帮助确定下一步行动。于是我启动了一个信赖的人工智能工具，首先询问关于针对客户的这类索赔的基本情况。说它比知识产权纠纷简单得多毫不为过。我的下一步是询问人工智能，对于“阻止”针对客户的诉讼提交，最好的方法是什么。人工智能返回了几种不同的策略，包括一些客户无法执行的（例如，根据企业规模获得安全港豁免资格）。我一直知道自己需要上传客户收到的请求函才能获得更有针对性的建议——但我认为首先从人工智能那里获得一些更一般的指导很重要，这样我就可以检查它是否给出前后一致的回复。

接下来，我上传了对方律师的请求函。人工智能在几秒钟内就完成了分析——甚至还调出了对方律师向州当局提交的原始通知——然后返回了一份详细的、分步骤的方法来指导客户了解其法律选项。对于第一步，我需要确认相关时期内销售了多少台产品。通过与客户快速邮件往来，我获得了这些信息。有了这些数据，人工智能就能够为客户准备更详细的方法来应对与对方律师的电话会议。它甚至建议了一个初始和解报价，足够高以迫使对方律师做出合理回应，但又足够低以反映客户即使被起诉也面临的有限风险。然后我转换方向，提示人工智能根据对方律师信函中列出的所有对其有利的事实，计算出客户在法律下的最大风险敞口。它确认，对客户最大的风险是卷入一场昂贵的法律程序，即使在实体问题上以很小差距败诉也可能导致一笔刺痛的律师费判决。

掌握了所有这些分析，加上我对人工智能建议的所有内容的独立核实，我随后才有信心就如何进行初始和解谈判向客户提供建议。我估计，即使那次谈话被证明不成功，我们也会通过讨论了解对方的一些情况，从而能够根据需要转向雇用有能力的当地辩护律师的原始策略。如果人工智能辅助的方法被证明有效，那么客户——即使在支付我的咨询费用后——也将节省大量的外部律师费，同时避免对业务的持续干扰。此外，由于客户传统上按项目固定费用与我合作，我不必太担心使用人工智能可能（或可能没有）为我节省了多少时间。相反，我和客户都将从人工智能的研究能力和它快速提供不同方法建议的速度中受益。正是在此基础上，加上我对它所指出的所有内容进行的独立确认，我的法律判断才得以介入，使我能够以我认为最好的方式为客户提供建议。

最终，我会将这次经历定性为人工智能的成功应用，无论该纠纷是以更快还是更慢的方式解决。也许这次经历对我来说最有趣的是，这不是一个我会放心委托给初级律师的项目，无论他们是否使用人工智能。如果他们不使用人工智能，我不会期望一位知识产权初级律师在我俩都不熟悉的法律领域给我提供准确的指导。如果他们使用人工智能，让他们向我复述人工智能的输出也不会有太大帮助——而且可能效率更低，出错几率更高。换句话说，就像我不会用初级律师的判断来替代我自己的判断一样，我也不会将我的判断外包给任何人工智能工具。展望未来，我会从我的业务中寻找一个例子，看看将人工智能辅助的任务交给初级律师会是更好的方法。与此同时，在这个项目上使用人工智能已经是我所能要求的一切了。

欢迎通过 gaston@k2k.law 或 Twitter：@gkroub 向我发送评论或问题。任何主题建议或想法都非常欢迎。

加斯顿·克鲁布住在布鲁克林，是 K2K IP Law 的创始合伙人，这是一家知识产权诉讼精品律所，也作为投资界专利问题的主要咨询机构。加斯顿的业务专注于知识产权诉讼及相关咨询，尤其侧重于专利事务。您可以通过 gaston@k2k.law 或 Twitter：@gkroub 联系他。

这篇文章首次发布于 Above the Law。
articles.summary:
It was the second non-IP request of the week from a longtime client, made through their new in-house counsel. The first of the two requests was easy enough to dispatch, even though it involved an already-filed litigation. The client and I had gotten a similar case dismissed a few years back, even before answering the complaint. All I needed to do was go back through my emails around that dispute. Once I refreshed my recollection on the investigation we had done in that earlier case, I was able to guide my client’s new in-house person as to what information he needed from the business unit and a relevant outside vendor. With that info in hand, it was then a simple matter of presenting it to opposing counsel and securing their agreement to drop the case. This time, we didn’t even need to engage Biglaw co-counsel with specific expertise in this type of litigation, as we did the first time around. Score one for the value of long-term client-attorney relationships and institutional knowledge.

The second matter that arose, however, was a bit more challenging. To start, the client had received a notice of the potential claim a while back, with a limited time remaining to reply. Complicating matters, the likelihood of the client getting indemnified by its supplier was nonexistent, since the product had been discontinued and recent sales were just an attempt to clear out remaining inventory. Moreover, the plaintiff’s attorney was threatening to file a lawsuit in state court, across the country from where my client is located, which would necessitate me helping the client identify competent local counsel in an area of law I was unfamiliar with. Gulping down this witches’ brew was an unattractive proposition on my part, especially because the client was asking for immediate help preparing for a last-ditch effort to get the matter resolved prelawsuit via a scheduled call with plaintiff’s counsel. Because the matter was time-sensitive, I did a quick read of opposing counsel’s demand letter and followed up with my client to confirm a factual point the letter raised. Because of my unfamiliarity with the type of claim, my plan was to suggest to the client that they ask around for recommendations to a firm based in the state where the dispute was centered.

In fact, that was my initial recommendation to the client by email. To the client’s credit, they pressed for more guidance, which prompted me to see what AI could do to help direct me to next steps. So I fired up one of my trusty AI tools and started by asking for a primer on the type of claim being raised against my client. To say it was nowhere as complex as an IP dispute would be an understatement. My next step was to ask the AI what would be the best approach to “head off” a filing against my client. The AI returned a few different strategies, including some that my client could not execute on, (e.g., qualifying for a safe harbor based on the size of the business, as one example). I knew all along that I would need to upload the demand letter my client had received to get more tailored advice — but I felt that it was important to get some more general guidance from the AI first, so that I could double check that it was giving me consistent responses.

As a next step, I uploaded the demand letter from opposing counsel. The AI analyzed it in seconds — and even pulled the underlying notice that opposing counsel had filed with the state authority — before returning a detailed, step-by-step approach to counseling the client on their legal options. For step one, I needed to confirm how many units had been sold in the relevant period. A quick email exchange with the client later, and I had the information. Armed with that data, the AI was able to prepare a more detailed approach for my client to take on the call with opposing counsel. It even suggested an opening settlement offer, large enough to force opposing counsel’s hand toward a reasonable response, but low enough to reflect the limited exposure my client faced even if a case was filed. I then switched gears, prompting the AI to give me my client’s maximum exposure under the law, taking all facts in the favor of opposing counsel as set forth in their letter. It confirmed that the biggest risk to my client was getting entangled in an expensive legal proceeding, where even a narrow loss on the merits could result in a stinging attorney’s fees award. 

With all that analysis in hand, coupled with my own verification of everything the AI had suggested, I then felt comfortable advising the client on how to approach the initial settlement discussion. I reckoned that even were that talk proven unsuccessful, we would have learned something about the other side by virtue of the discussion, with the ability to pivot to the original strategy of hiring competent local defense counsel as needed. And if our AI-assisted approach proves to work, then the client — even after paying me for my counsel — would have saved significant money in outside counsel fees, while avoiding an ongoing business distraction with nothing to gain. Moreover, because the client has traditionally worked with me on a flat fee per project basis, I didn’t have to worry too much about how much time using AI may (or may not) have saved me. Instead, both the client and I would have benefited by the research capabilities of the AI, as well as the speed with which it suggested different approaches that I was allowed to pressure test. Only on that foundation, as well as my confirmation with independent research of everything it pointed me to, did my legal judgment kick in to allow me to advise the client in the way I felt best.

Ultimately, I would couch this experience as a successful use of AI, irrespective of whether this dispute resolved on a faster or slower track. Perhaps what is most interesting to me about the experience is the fact that this was not a project that I would have felt comfortable delegating to an associate, whether or not they used AI. If they didn’t use AI, I wouldn’t expect an IP associate to give me accurate guidance on an area of law unfamiliar to us both. And if they did use AI, having them regurgitate for me the AI’s output would have been of little additional help — and likely more inefficient with a higher chance of error. Put another way, just as I would not substitute an associate’s judgment for my own, neither would I outsource my judgment to any AI tool. Going forward, I will look for an example from my practice where handing off an AI-assisted assignment an associate’s way would prove to have been a better approach. In the meantime, using AI on this one project was all I could ask for.
